name: Hydrogen
description: Lightest element; fuels stars via fusion; highly flammable diatomic gas

name: Helium
description: Inert noble gas; second most abundant element in universe; used in cryogenics

name: Lithium
description: Lightest metal; used in batteries and psychiatric medication; reacts with water

name: Beryllium
description: Lightweight structural metal; toxic dust causes berylliosis; very stiff

name: Boron
description: Metalloid used in glass and ceramics; extremely hard; neutron absorber

name: Carbon
description: Basis of organic chemistry; forms diamond and graphite allotropes

name: Nitrogen
description: Diatomic gas making up 78% of atmosphere; essential for amino acids

name: Oxygen
description: Diatomic gas essential for respiration and combustion; 21% of atmosphere

name: Fluorine
description: Most electronegative element; extremely reactive pale yellow gas; toxic

name: Neon
description: Inert noble gas; produces reddish-orange glow in discharge tubes

name: Sodium
description: Soft reactive metal; essential nutrient; reacts violently with water

name: Magnesium
description: Light structural metal; burns with bright white flame; essential mineral

name: Aluminum
description: Most abundant metal in crust; lightweight; excellent conductor

name: Silicon
description: Semiconductor basis of modern electronics; second most abundant element in crust

name: Phosphorus
description: Essential for DNA and ATP; white phosphorus is pyrophoric and toxic

name: Sulfur
description: Yellow nonmetal; used in gunpowder and vulcanization; essential nutrient

name: Chlorine
description: Greenish-yellow toxic gas; strong oxidizer; used in water purification

name: Argon
description: Third most abundant gas in atmosphere; used in welding and lighting

name: Potassium
description: Essential nutrient; reacts violently with water; soft silvery metal

name: Calcium
description: Essential for bones and teeth; fifth most abundant element in crust

name: Scandium
description: Light transition metal; used in aerospace alloys; rare

name: Titanium
description: Strong lightweight corrosion-resistant metal; used in aerospace and implants

name: Vanadium
description: Hard steel-gray metal; used in tool steels; toxic compounds

name: Chromium
description: Hard lustrous metal; used for plating and stainless steel; Cr(VI) is carcinogenic

name: Manganese
description: Essential trace element; used in steel production; hard brittle metal

name: Iron
description: Most used metal; essential for hemoglobin; basis of steel

name: Cobalt
description: Hard lustrous metal; used in magnets and batteries; blue pigment

name: Nickel
description: Corrosion-resistant metal; used in stainless steel and coins; skin allergen

name: Copper
description: Excellent electrical conductor; essential trace element; reddish metal

name: Zinc
description: Corrosion-resistant coating metal; essential trace element; bluish-white

name: Gallium
description: Melts near body temperature; used in semiconductors and LEDs

name: Germanium
description: Semiconductor; used in fiber optics and infrared optics

name: Arsenic
description: Toxic metalloid; historically used as poison; used in semiconductors

name: Selenium
description: Essential trace element in small amounts; toxic in excess; photoconductor

name: Bromine
description: One of two liquid elements at STP; reddish-brown; corrosive and toxic vapors

name: Krypton
description: Noble gas; used in high-performance lighting and photography flash

name: Rubidium
description: Soft silvery metal; ignites in air; used in atomic clocks

name: Strontium
description: Produces red flame; used in fireworks; chemically similar to calcium

name: Yttrium
description: Used in LEDs and laser crystals; named after Ytterby village in Sweden

name: Zirconium
description: Corrosion-resistant; used in nuclear reactors and surgical instruments

name: Niobium
description: Superconducting metal; used in jet engines and MRI magnets

name: Molybdenum
description: High melting point metal; essential trace element; used in steel alloys

name: Technetium
description: First artificially produced element; used in medical imaging; all isotopes radioactive

name: Ruthenium
description: Hard platinum group metal; used in electronics and catalysis; toxic oxide

name: Rhodium
description: Rare precious metal; used in catalytic converters; highly reflective

name: Palladium
description: Precious metal; absorbs hydrogen; used in catalytic converters and electronics

name: Silver
description: Highest electrical and thermal conductivity of any element; precious metal

name: Cadmium
description: Toxic heavy metal; used in batteries and pigments; carcinogenic

name: Indium
description: Soft metal; used in touchscreens and solders; makes a cry when bent

name: Tin
description: Used in solder and tin cans; two allotropes (white and gray tin)

name: Antimony
description: Brittle metalloid; used in flame retardants and lead-acid batteries

name: Tellurium
description: Rare metalloid; used in solar cells and thermoelectric devices

name: Iodine
description: Essential trace element for thyroid; violet vapor; used as antiseptic

name: Xenon
description: Noble gas; used in ion propulsion engines and high-intensity lamps

name: Cesium
description: Most electropositive stable element; used in atomic clocks; reacts explosively with water

name: Barium
description: Produces green flame; barium compounds used in X-ray imaging; toxic soluble salts

name: Lanthanum
description: First lanthanide; used in camera lenses and catalytic converters

name: Cerium
description: Most abundant rare earth; pyrophoric when finely divided; used in lighter flints

name: Praseodymium
description: Used in magnets and green glass; named for its green salts

name: Neodymium
description: Used in powerful permanent magnets (NdFeB); pink-purple salts

name: Promethium
description: Only radioactive lanthanide with no stable isotopes; used in luminous paint

name: Samarium
description: Used in samarium-cobalt magnets; neutron absorber

name: Europium
description: Most reactive rare earth; used in red phosphors for displays

name: Gadolinium
description: Highest neutron absorption of any element; used as MRI contrast agent

name: Terbium
description: Used in green phosphors and solid-state devices; magnetostrictive

name: Dysprosium
description: Used in nuclear control rods and magnets; highly magnetic

name: Holmium
description: Highest magnetic moment of any element; used in lasers

name: Erbium
description: Pink-colored salts; used in fiber optic amplifiers

name: Thulium
description: Rarest naturally occurring lanthanide; used in portable X-ray sources

name: Ytterbium
description: Soft metal; used in stress gauges and metallurgy

name: Lutetium
description: Hardest and densest lanthanide; used in PET scan detectors

name: Hafnium
description: Used in nuclear reactor control rods; always found with zirconium

name: Tantalum
description: Very hard corrosion-resistant metal; used in capacitors and surgical implants

name: Tungsten
description: Highest melting point of all metals; used in light bulb filaments and cutting tools

name: Rhenium
description: One of rarest elements in crust; used in jet engine superalloys

name: Osmium
description: Densest naturally occurring element; toxic tetroxide; hard and brittle

name: Iridium
description: Most corrosion-resistant metal; used in spark plugs and crucibles

name: Platinum
description: Precious catalyst metal; used in catalytic converters and jewelry

name: Gold
description: Precious metal; excellent conductor; does not tarnish; monetary standard

name: Mercury
description: Only metal liquid at STP; toxic; used in thermometers and barometers

name: Thallium
description: Extremely toxic; historically used as rat poison; tasteless and odorless salts

name: Lead
description: Dense toxic metal; used in batteries and radiation shielding; neurotoxin

name: Bismuth
description: Least toxic heavy metal; used in medicine (Pepto-Bismol); iridescent oxide layer

name: Polonium
description: Intensely radioactive; alpha emitter; used in antistatic devices; extremely toxic

name: Astatine
description: Rarest naturally occurring element; radioactive halogen; very short-lived

name: Radon
description: Radioactive noble gas; lung cancer risk from indoor accumulation

name: Francium
description: Most unstable of first 101 elements; extremely radioactive alkali metal

name: Radium
description: Radioactive; glows faintly blue; historically used in luminous paint

name: Actinium
description: Radioactive; glows blue in dark due to intense radioactivity

name: Thorium
description: Radioactive; potential nuclear fuel; more abundant than uranium

name: Protactinium
description: Radioactive; rare; toxic and radiotoxic

name: Uranium
description: Primary nuclear fuel; dense; radioactive with very long half-life

name: Neptunium
description: First transuranium element; produced in nuclear reactors; radioactive

name: Plutonium
description: Nuclear weapons and reactor fuel; extremely toxic and radioactive; pyrophoric

name: Americium
description: Used in smoke detectors; radioactive; produced in nuclear reactors

name: Curium
description: Named after Marie and Pierre Curie; intensely radioactive; glows red

name: Berkelium
description: Named after Berkeley California; produced in microgram quantities

name: Californium
description: Strong neutron emitter; used in nuclear reactor startup and cancer treatment

name: Einsteinium
description: Named after Einstein; produced in thermonuclear explosions; very short-lived

name: Fermium
description: Named after Enrico Fermi; produced in nuclear explosions; no bulk uses

name: Mendelevium
description: Named after Mendeleev; only produced in atom quantities

name: Nobelium
description: Named after Alfred Nobel; most stable isotope has 58-minute half-life

name: Lawrencium
description: Last actinide; named after Ernest Lawrence; very short-lived

name: Rutherfordium
description: First transactinide; named after Ernest Rutherford; seconds-long half-life

name: Dubnium
description: Named after Dubna Russia; produced only in particle accelerators

name: Seaborgium
description: Named after Glenn Seaborg; half-life of minutes

name: Bohrium
description: Named after Niels Bohr; extremely short-lived synthetic element

name: Hassium
description: Named after Hesse Germany; predicted to be very dense

name: Meitnerium
description: Named after Lise Meitner; only a few atoms ever produced

name: Darmstadtium
description: Named after Darmstadt Germany; extremely short-lived

name: Roentgenium
description: Named after Wilhelm Roentgen; predicted to resemble gold

name: Copernicium
description: Named after Copernicus; may be a gas or volatile liquid at STP

name: Nihonium
description: Named after Japan (Nihon); first element discovered in Asia

name: Flerovium
description: Named after Flerov Laboratory; may behave as noble gas

name: Moscovium
description: Named after Moscow Oblast; superheavy element with very short half-life

name: Livermorium
description: Named after Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory

name: Tennessine
description: Named after Tennessee; superheavy halogen; millisecond half-life

name: Oganesson
description: Heaviest known element; named after Yuri Oganessian; predicted noble gas properties
